Saint Peter in Chains is the sole unmatched item.

entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk for Horstmann’s sixty-five-item selection
notes: The sixty-five Horstmann item count is retained. DIMEV record boundaries are collateral identifiers, not replacement item boundaries.
The eight-line uncounted Northern prologue matches record-2003 / wit-2003-1 and remains item_count 0.
Records 755 and 960 occur only as Middle English quotations in Horstmann’s introductory front matter; they are not silently added to the sixty-five counted text items.

Boundary overlaps are explicit: record-2652 spans the separately modeled Sixtus and Lawrence items; record-383 spans the Vernon Ipotis and Caligula Ypotys versions; record-317 spans the Ashmole William Basterdfeld text and Thornton Lamentacio; the Ashmole Peter-and-Paul sermon contains record-2591 inside record-5800; the combined Katherine item maps to records 1882 and 1881; and Horstmann’s Edmund/Fremund item maps to five records.

DIMEV’s Horstmann1881a references contain several anomalies checked against the edition and source incipits: record-364 gives pp. 22–35 for the pp. 225–235 Auchinleck Margaret; record-5690 gives p. 186 for the pp. 147–152 All Souls item; record-5800 attaches pp. 77–92 to the Huntington row although Horstmann’s selected sermon is Ashmole 42 at pp. 77–81; and record-3348 omits Horstmann despite an exact Ashmole 61 incipit, locus, and text match.

lineage.summary: The repository XML derives from U-M’s 2006 uncorrected OCR/TEI encoding of Carl Horstmann’s 1881 multi-witness edited selection. This graph distinguishes the repository artifact, encoding, complete printed edition, U-M and Internet Archive surrogates, direct manuscript/early-print sources, comparison witnesses, the Hickes intermediary, a reported lost Trinity witness, and the DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk. Ten cached objects contain reproducible target portions; seven supporting/catalog or zero-overlap objects explicitly mark the target absent.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval authors; John Lydgate for three scoped items
catalogSummary.date: Medieval texts of varied dates; edited selection first published in 1881
catalogSummary.region: England and Scotland; composition and dialect regions vary by item
catalogSummary.title: Altenglische Legenden: Neue Folge

catalogingSubject.label: Altenglische Legenden: Neue Folge
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is Carl Horstmann’s 1881 edited selection of sixty-five medieval verse items, not one medieval work, one manuscript, or the complete contents of every cited witness. The German editorial prose, source notes, alternate witness versions, and a poem embedded in the back notes remain explicit parts of this edition-level boundary.

extent.description: Sixty-five cataloged medieval verse items. The raw XML contains 11 DIV1, 66 DIV2, 37 DIV3, 1,013 LG, and 34,045 L elements. The L total includes an eight-line uncounted prologue framing the Northern Legendary; the item-level metrics sum to 34,037 lines. The three zero-line numbered headings for Purification, Annunciation, and Nativity of John are German editorial notices, not medieval text items.

formStatements.description: The cataloging subject is intentionally Horstmann’s edited selection as a whole, which retains sixty-five verse items together with substantial German editorial prose, apparatus, and source commentary. Every medieval item part is separately typed as verse.

scope.description: All sixty-five cataloged medieval text items.
Dominant but not exclusive: saints’ lives and feast legends are interspersed with devotional, visionary, and exemplary verse.
The medieval verse items other than the explicitly Older Scots Saint Machor.
Horstmann’s titles, introductions, source notes, and apparatus.
Rubrics, titles, source quotations, and short embedded passages; no separately cataloged Latin work item is present.

notes: The repository XML language header says only ger; this record corrects that encoding-level simplification by cataloging substantive Middle English, Older Scots, editorial German, and embedded Latin separately.

The three zero-line numbered headings at Purification, Annunciation, and the Nativity of John the Baptist are excluded from the item count because they contain German editorial source notes but no encoded medieval text.
John Barbour appears in inherited U-M bibliographic metadata and Horstmann’s historical collection label; neither is treated as secure whole-selection or Saint Machor authorship.
The XML milestone 'Nero CXII fol. 181' is preserved as legacy evidence, but the current British Library component record maps the Wulfhad and Ruffin text to Cotton Nero C XII, ff. 183r–188v.

The sixty-five text items are cataloged item/version streams within this edited selection. The count is not an assertion that every stream is a wholly distinct abstract medieval work: alternate Alexius, Katherine, and Edmund/Fremund recensions are deliberately not double-counted, while independently presented hell stories and Marian fragments are counted separately.

The complete DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk checks all sixty-five counted verse items: sixty-four match sixty-seven unique records through seventy part-to-record mappings, while Saint Peter in Chains remains explicitly unmatched. Record-2003 maps the uncounted eight-line prologue; records 755 and 960 are front-matter quotations and do not alter the sixty-five-item boundary.

summaries.value: Carl Horstmann’s 1881 Altenglische Legenden: Neue Folge is cataloged here as a multi-witness scholarly edited selection containing sixty-five medieval verse text items alongside retained German editorial prose, source notes, and textual apparatus. Its intellectual boundary is the edition’s selected streams and recensions, not one medieval work or one manuscript.

Horstmann’s volume brings together a Northern Legendary sequence, two first-appendix lives, a broad group of single legends, a final appendix, and one independent Thornton poem embedded in the notes. The sixty-five-item count treats separately presented intellectual texts as items, counts the two hell stories and two Marian fragments separately, but does not double-count alternate versions of Alexius, Katherine, or Edmund and Fremund. A complete DIMEV v1.0 crosswalk matches sixty-four of the sixty-five items to sixty-seven unique records through seventy part-to-record mappings; repertory overlaps are recorded without replacing the edition-defined item boundary.

The U-M page-image object has 708 canvas positions but exactly 674 unique image payloads, reconciling the XML header’s 674 source TIFF images. It includes omitted front matter, the unencoded Early Modern Saint Eustace on printed pp. 472–487, and unencoded notes, corrigenda, and contents; these image-layer materials are not silently promoted into the sixty-five encoded medieval text items. Likewise, DIMEV record-2003 identifies the uncounted eight-line prologue and records 755 and 960 identify front-matter quotations, none of which changes the count.

notes: The preferred title follows Horstmann’s title page. In this nineteenth-century usage, Altenglisch encompasses texts now cataloged as Middle English and Older Scots; it does not mean that the contents are Old English.
A literal supplied English translation of the printed German title, not a claim that the medieval texts are in Old English.
